category_title: High school students with LD and ADHD who struggle to break down multi-step algebraic tasks and lack confidence in their accuracy
scenario_title: Multiplying linear expressions using abstract notation
minimal_context: The student is working on multiplying the linear expressions (x + 3) and (x + 5) using only abstract notation during a one-on-one tutoring session.

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: Let's try multiplying (x + 3) and (x + 5) using only the abstract symbols today, moving away from the blocks.

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: It’s so much more hard because it’s not broken up then.

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: Just do your best to work through the problem.

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: I can’t break it down. I guess I’m not good at blending the steps.

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: Write down what you think the answer is.

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: (Writes x2 + 15) I got x2 + 15. But when it’s all numbers then I forgot where to break it up and what’s what.

dialogue_combined:
TUTOR: Let's try multiplying (x + 3) and (x + 5) using only the abstract symbols today, moving away from the blocks.

STUDENT: It’s so much more hard because it’s not broken up then.

TUTOR: Just do your best to work through the problem.

STUDENT: I can’t break it down. I guess I’m not good at blending the steps.

TUTOR: Write down what you think the answer is.

STUDENT: (Writes x2 + 15) I got x2 + 15. But when it’s all numbers then I forgot where to break it up and what’s what.
